content.full_text:
June 22, 1990

Mr. Kevin Braaten-Moen  
Technical Assistance and Analysis Division  
Fair Political Practices Commission  
P.O. Box 807  
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Re: Lobbying Services Donated by One Lobbyist Employer to Another Lobbyist Employer

Dear Kevin:

Thank you for your assistance. This letter confirms our telephone conversation of June 21, 1990.

California Farm Bureau Federation ("Farm Bureau") is a voluntary membership non-profit organization. It is also a lobbyist employer, employing lobbyists who represent its members' interests before the State Legislature and the California Public Utilities Commission ("PUC"). Farm Bureau has offered to donate the services of one of its lobbyists for the purpose of representing the interests of another voluntary membership non-profit organization before the PUC, and possibly before the State Legislature when bills related to energy regulation are under consideration. The entity which will benefit from these donated services is itself a lobbyist employer with lobbyists representing its members' interests in non-energy matters before the State Legislature. It has no lobbyists representing it before the PUC. Neither Farm Bureau nor its lobbyist employee whose services will be donated will receive any compensation for, or reimbursement of expenses related to, these activities. However, the lobbyist employee will periodically meet with the board of directors and members of the entity receiving the donated services, and Farm Bureau will be reimbursed for the travel, meal, and lodging expenses directly attributable to these meetings.

I requested your advice concerning the reporting responsibilities of both lobbyist employers and the individual lobbyist involved. I understand your advice to be as follows:

1. Neither Farm Bureau as a lobbyist employer, nor the

lobbyist providing the donated services, will incur any reporting responsibilities related to these activities. The reimbursement of expenses related solely to the lobbyist's meetings with the board of directors and members of the entity receiving the donated services does not constitute compensation for lobbying services.

2. The entity receiving the donated services does incur reporting responsibilities. It must disclose on its own lobbyist employer report, the PUC application numbers and legislative bills concerning which its interests were represented by Farm Bureau's lobbyist. It must also report its reimbursement of expenses related to the meetings between the lobbyist and its board of directors or members as "other payments to influence legislative or administrative action" on Schedule D of its lobbyist employer report.

Thank you for a written response.

Yours very truly,

[Signature]
Carolyn S. Richardson
Associate Counsel

cc: Bob L. Vice
    George Gomes
    James Eller
    David Moore
    Jasper Hempel
